title: What is a data type
instruction: Every variable has a type that determines what kind of data it holds — like different library shelves for different kinds of books. Run this and see three different types in action.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	title := "The Great Gatsby"
	copies := 12
	available := true
	fmt.Println(title)
	fmt.Println(copies)
	fmt.Println(available)
}
successMessage: Three variables, three types. Each one holds a different kind of data — just like different shelves hold different kinds of books.

title: string — text in quotes
instruction: A `string` holds text — book titles, author names, ISBNs. Always wrapped in double quotes. Create a variable called `bookTitle` with any book title and print it.
hint: bookTitle := "The Hobbit"  — text always goes inside double quotes.

codeChecks.message: Create a string variable: bookTitle := "The Hobbit"
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Strings are the most common type you will use. Any text — always in double quotes.

title: int and float64 — numbers
instruction: `int` holds whole numbers (no decimal). `float64` holds numbers with decimals. Create `totalCopies` as int (42) and `bookPrice` as float64 (14.99). Print both.
hint: totalCopies := 42  and  bookPrice := 14.99  — no quotes around numbers.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: int for whole numbers, float64 for decimals. Go picks the right type automatically based on the value.

title: bool — true or false
instruction: A `bool` holds exactly one of two values: `true` or `false`. No quotes. Create `isCheckedOut := true` and `isReferenceOnly := false`. Print both.
hint: isCheckedOut := true  and  isReferenceOnly := false  — no quotes around true or false.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Bools power every decision in your program. Every if, every condition — it all comes down to true or false.

title: Type inference — Go figures it out
instruction: You have been using `:=` without writing the type name. Go reads the value and picks the type automatically. Run this and see the types Go assigned using `%T` in `fmt.Printf`.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	title     := "The Great Gatsby"
	copies    := 12
	price     := 14.99
	available := true
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", title)
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", copies)
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", price)
	fmt.Printf("%T\n", available)
}
successMessage: Go reads your value and picks the right type automatically. You only need to be explicit when you want a specific sized type.

title: "42" is not 42 — quotes matter
instruction: `42` is a number you can do math with. `"42"` is a string — the characters 4 and 2. The code below tracks checked-out books but has a bug. Fix `b` so both numbers add up correctly.
hint: Remove the quotes around 42 on the second line. A number should never have quotes around it.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	a := 10
	b := "42"
	fmt.Println(a + b)
}

codeChecks.message: Remove the quotes from 42. It should be a number: b := 42

successMessage: Quotes mean text. No quotes means number. Never mix them — your library's checkout counts depend on getting this right.

title: Type conversion — change types explicitly
instruction: Go will not automatically mix types. Convert with `float64(myInt)` or `int(myFloat)`. Fix the code by converting `booksOut` to `float64` before adding it to `lateFee`.
hint: Change the Println line to: fmt.Println(float64(booksOut) + lateFee)
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	booksOut := 3
	lateFee  := 1.50
	fmt.Println(booksOut + lateFee)
}

codeChecks.message: Convert booksOut to float64 before adding: float64(booksOut) + lateFee

successMessage: Go never converts types silently. You are always in control of when and how a conversion happens.

title: Use six types in one program
instruction: Create six variables using six different types: `sectionName` (string), `totalShelves` (int), `members` (int64), `lateFeeRate` (float64), `isOpen` (bool), `sectionCode` (byte = 'F'). Print all six.
hint: sectionName := "Fiction", totalShelves := 15, var members int64 = 2500, lateFeeRate := 0.50, isOpen := true, var sectionCode byte = 'F'

codeChecks.message: Create members as int64 using the var keyword
Create lateFeeRate as a float64 with a decimal value
Create sectionCode as a byte set to a letter like 'F'

successMessage: Six types, one program. You now know every basic data type in Go — and how to track a whole library with them.
